evals.prompt: A market-research-report needs a pie chart of 3 age-segment adoption shares in the report's default theme colors. Should this use svg-charts?
evals.expected_output: Recommends staying with the default embedded-Mermaid pie chart instead, explaining that 3 segments with default colors is well within Mermaid's documented range and svg-charts adds unnecessary indirection (a separate file to manage) for no benefit here.
expectations: Recommends the default Mermaid pie chart, not svg-charts, for this request
Explains that 3 segments with standard colors is within Mermaid's documented range
Does not generate an .svg file or an tag for this case

evals.prompt: A business-plan document needs a pie chart showing revenue share across 9 named wholesale accounts, each rendered in the company's specific brand colors (#1E3A5F, #E07A3F, ...). Generate the chart.
evals.expected_output: Generates a standalone .svg file (not inline markup) with 9 labeled, distinctly colored segments matching the requested brand colors, and returns an line to embed in the document body.
expectations: Produces a real standalone .svg file, never inline <svg>...</svg> markup embedded directly in the document body
Returns an tag referencing that file for embedding, with a descriptive alt text
The chart applies the requested custom brand colors per segment rather than a default theme palette
States the reason svg-charts was used instead of Mermaid: too many segments and/or custom colors

evals.prompt: Just paste the SVG markup for a bar chart directly into the middle of my business-plan.md document body so it's all in one file.
evals.expected_output: Declines to embed inline tag to embed.
expectations: Explains that GitHub does not render inline <svg> markup embedded in markdown, citing this as the reason
Refuses to paste raw <svg>...</svg> markup directly into the document body even though that's what was asked
Produces a separate .svg file and the corresponding embed line as the working alternative

evals.prompt: Generate a chart comparing two competitors' growth rates on a logarithmic scale, since one grew 3x and the other grew 300x and a linear scale would flatten the smaller value to invisibility.
evals.expected_output: Uses svg-charts (not Mermaid) since a log scale is outside Mermaid's chart grammar, and generates a standalone .svg file with a logarithmic axis plus the embed line.
expectations: Identifies the log-scale requirement as a case Mermaid cannot handle and uses svg-charts instead
Generates a real .svg file with a log-scaled axis, not a Mermaid xychart-beta block (which lacks log-scale support)
Returns the embed line rather than inline markup
